(for sponsorship reasons called **[PENNY](/wiki/Penny_(supermarket) "Penny (supermarket)") Deutsche Eishockey Liga**) (German pronunciation: [[ˌdɔʏtʃə ˈʔaɪshɔki ˌliːɡa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"); English: *German Ice Hockey League*) or **DEL**, is a professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") league in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and the highest division in German ice hockey. Founded in 1994, it was formed as a replacement for the [Eishockey-Bundesliga](/wiki/Eishockey-Bundesliga "Eishockey-Bundesliga") and became the new top-tier league in Germany as a result. Unlike the old Bundesliga, the DEL is not under the administration of the [German Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/Deutscher_Eishockey-Bund "Deutscher Eishockey-Bund").

The **Deutsche Eishockey Liga** (for sponsorship reasons called **[PENNY](/wiki/Penny_(supermarket) "Penny (supermarket)") Deutsche Eishockey Liga**) (German pronunciation: [[ˌdɔʏtʃə ˈʔaɪshɔki ˌliːɡa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"); English: *German Ice Hockey League*) or **DEL**, is a professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") league in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and the highest division in German ice hockey. Founded in 1994, it was formed as a replacement for the [Eishockey-Bundesliga](/wiki/Eishockey-Bundesliga "Eishockey-Bundesliga") and became the new top-tier league in Germany as a result. Unlike the old Bundesliga, the DEL is not under the administration of the [German Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/Deutscher_Eishockey-Bund "Deutscher Eishockey-Bund").

Teams from the DEL participate in the [IIHF](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation")'s annual [Champions Hockey League](/wiki/Champions_Hockey_League "Champions Hockey League") (CHL), competing for the European Trophy. Participation is based on the strength of the various leagues in Europe (excluding the European/Asian [Kontinental Hockey League](/wiki/Kontinental_Hockey_League "Kontinental Hockey League")). Going into the [2022–23 CHL season](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Champions_Hockey_League "2022–23 Champions Hockey League"), the DEL was [ranked the No. 3 league](/wiki/Champions_Hockey_League#League_ranking "Champions Hockey League") in Europe, allowing them to send their top four teams to compete in the CHL.

In the 2016–17 season, the league was the second-best supported ice hockey league in Europe, behind the Swiss [National League A](/wiki/National_League_A "National League A"), with an average attendance of 6,198 spectators per game.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Fourteen different teams comprise the league, playing their home games in a diverse mix of venues, including a few large, modern arenas (e.g. in Berlin, Mannheim, Cologne, and Düsseldorf) and older, smaller venues (e.g. in Iserlohn, Schwenningen and Straubing). [Kölner Haie](/wiki/K%C3%B6lner_Haie "Kölner Haie") (English: Cologne Sharks), [Düsseldorfer EG](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorfer_EG "Düsseldorfer EG"), and [Eisbären Berlin](/wiki/Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin "Eisbären Berlin") (Berlin Polar Bears) all regularly attract over 15,000 fans for home games. [Adler Mannheim](/wiki/Adler_Mannheim "Adler Mannheim") (Mannheim Eagles) also regularly attract over 12,000 fans per home game with each of their home games in the 2019 DEL Playoffs, including all three games in the final series against [EHC Red Bull München](/wiki/EHC_Red_Bull_M%C3%BCnchen "EHC Red Bull München"), selling out. Despite attracting some of the biggest crowds in the DEL, and hosting [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") teams in preseason games, Kölner Haie have not won a championship since 2002.

In the DEL Winter Game, similar to the [NHL Winter Classic](/wiki/NHL_Winter_Classic "NHL Winter Classic") where matches are played in larger outdoor venues, Cologne and Düsseldorf have played in front of crowds in excess of 40,000, notably in 2019 as Düsseldorf won in overtime in rival Cologne's [RheinEnergieStadion](/wiki/RheinEnergieStadion "RheinEnergieStadion"). The DEL Winter Game traditionally takes place every two years; four editions have been played since 2013, including games in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") and at the home of [TSG Hoffenheim](/wiki/TSG_Hoffenheim "TSG Hoffenheim"), with each attracting over 30,000 spectators. Following a 2021 postponement due to the ongoing [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the DEL Winter Game returned in December 2022 with Cologne hosting Mannheim in the RheinEnergieStadion.

The DEL is known for its game-day atmosphere, as well as producing NHL talents including [Dominik Kahun](/wiki/Dominik_Kahun "Dominik Kahun"), [Tim Stützle](/wiki/Tim_St%C3%BCtzle "Tim Stützle") and [Lukas Reichel](/wiki/Lukas_Reichel "Lukas Reichel"). Many of the German national team that [took silver](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Men's tournament") at the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics") in [Pyeongchang](/wiki/Pyeongchang "Pyeongchang") played in the DEL, while [EHC Red Bull München](/wiki/EHC_Red_Bull_M%C3%BCnchen "EHC Red Bull München") became the first German team to reach the [Champions Hockey League](/wiki/Champions_Hockey_League "Champions Hockey League") final in [2019](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Champions_Hockey_League "2018–19 Champions Hockey League"), losing to the Swedish team [Frölunda HC](/wiki/Fr%C3%B6lunda_HC "Frölunda HC"). [EHC Red Bull München](/wiki/EHC_Red_Bull_M%C3%BCnchen "EHC Red Bull München") followed this up by reaching the semi-finals in the [2021-22 Champions Hockey League](/wiki/2021-22_Champions_Hockey_League "2021-22 Champions Hockey League"). Ice hockey is growing as a sport in Germany and is seen as a popular alternative to [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") or [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), building upon the Olympic success in 2018.

A new system of promotion and relegation with the [DEL2](/wiki/DEL2 "DEL2") began with the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_DEL_season "2021–22 DEL season"), in which the last-placed DEL club will be replaced by the DEL2 champions. [SC Bietigheim Steelers](/wiki/SC_Bietigheim_Steelers "SC Bietigheim Steelers") joined the DEL for the [2021-22 DEL season](/wiki/2021-22_DEL_season "2021-22 DEL season") as DEL2 champions ahead of the first promotion/relegation to [DEL2](/wiki/DEL2 "DEL2"). Following the conclusion of the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_DEL_season "2021–22 DEL season"), Bietigheim retained their place in the top flight while [Krefeld Pinguine](/wiki/Krefeld_Pinguine "Krefeld Pinguine") were relegated after finishing bottom of the table. [Löwen Frankfurt](/wiki/L%C3%B6wen_Frankfurt "Löwen Frankfurt") (Frankfurt Lions) were promoted as [DEL2](/wiki/DEL2 "DEL2") champions for the [2022–23 season](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_DEL_season "2022–23 DEL season").

The DEL Playoffs decide the season champions using a format akin to that of the NHL. Each team plays 52 regular-season games to determine the regular-season champions before the top 10 teams are seeded and qualify for the playoffs. The top 6 teams receive a bye to the quarter-finals while the teams ranked 7–10 compete in the pre-playoff (wildcard) round. The seedings are re-calculated after each round in a straight knockout system. The DEL Finals, held in late April or early May, is a best-of-7 games format, similar to the [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") where the top seed remaining holds home-ice advantage in a Game 7 scenario.

For the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_DEL_season "2021–22 DEL season") and [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_DEL_season "2020–21 DEL season"), a shortened version of the DEL playoffs was used in light of the COVID-19 pandemic. [Eisbären Berlin](/wiki/Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin "Eisbären Berlin") won the 2022 Finals against [EHC Red Bull München](/wiki/EHC_Red_Bull_M%C3%BCnchen "EHC Red Bull München") 3-1 in a best-of-5 format. Berlin also won the title in 2021, defeating Wolfsburg 2-1 in a best-of-3 series following a North/South regional league split during the 2020/21 season due to the pandemic.

The [Eisbären Berlin](/wiki/Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin "Eisbären Berlin") and [Adler Mannheim](/wiki/Adler_Mannheim "Adler Mannheim") are the most successful DEL clubs and have won the last three editions between them (Mannheim 2019, Berlin 2021 & 2022). Mannheim have enjoyed a lot of success in recent years (2015 and 2019 Champions, 2021 Regular Season Champions) although [EHC Red Bull München](/wiki/EHC_Red_Bull_M%C3%BCnchen "EHC Red Bull München") won three consecutive titles between 2016 and 2018 and also made the 2019 final, losing to Mannheim in five games. München were also top of the 2020 standings before the season was curtailed. [ERC Ingolstadt](/wiki/ERC_Ingolstadt "ERC Ingolstadt") (2014 Champions as the 9th seed) and [Grizzlys Wolfsburg](/wiki/Grizzlys_Wolfsburg "Grizzlys Wolfsburg") (2021 runners-up) have also enjoyed deep playoff runs in the last decade.

Some of the league's biggest rivalries include [Kölner Haie](/wiki/K%C3%B6lner_Haie "Kölner Haie") and [Düsseldorfer EG](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorfer_EG "Düsseldorfer EG"), Kölner Haie and [Adler Mannheim](/wiki/Adler_Mannheim "Adler Mannheim"), and [Eisbären Berlin](/wiki/Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin "Eisbären Berlin") and [EHC Red Bull München](/wiki/EHC_Red_Bull_M%C3%BCnchen "EHC Red Bull München"). The Bavarian Derbies between München, [ERC Ingolstadt](/wiki/ERC_Ingolstadt "ERC Ingolstadt"), [Augsburger Panther](/wiki/Augsburger_Panther "Augsburger Panther"), [Straubing Tigers](/wiki/Straubing_Tigers "Straubing Tigers"), and [Nürnberg Ice Tigers](/wiki/N%C3%BCrnberg_Ice_Tigers "Nürnberg Ice Tigers") are also fiercely contested. Due to geography, Nürnberg/Straubing and Augsburg/Ingolstadt are notable rivalries although Munich/Augsburg has become a stronger rivalry following their epic 2019 semi-final series. München won that series in the deciding game 7 which also included the longest match in DEL history, won by [Augsburger Panther](/wiki/Augsburger_Panther "Augsburger Panther") after 104 minutes of action in game 3, as two matches required three overtimes to determine a winner.

The [Eishockey-Bundesliga](/wiki/Eishockey-Bundesliga "Eishockey-Bundesliga") was formed in 1957 as the elite hockey competition in the Federal Republic of Germany, replacing the *Oberliga* in this position. It was in turn replaced by the Deutsche Eishockey Liga, which now also carries the name **1st Bundesliga** in its logo.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7e/Deutsche_Eishockey-Liga_Logo_1994.png/250px-Deutsche_Eishockey-Liga_Logo_1994.png)](/wiki/File:Deutsche_Eishockey-Liga_Logo_1994.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga_Logo_2001.svg/250px-Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga_Logo_2001.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga_Logo_2001.svg)

Former DEL logo (2001–2011)

The DEL was founded in the 1994–95 season, consisting of teams from the Eishockey-Bundesliga's [1st](/wiki/Ice_hockey_Bundesliga "Ice hockey Bundesliga") and [2nd divisions](/wiki/2nd_Bundesliga_(ice_hockey) "2nd Bundesliga (ice hockey)"). The condition of these earlier leagues had become intolerable. Many 1st and 2nd division teams were heavily in debt. The 2nd division attracted few sponsors and spectators. As a result, many clubs were forced to fold or withdraw to the lower leagues. Fans and corporate sponsors focused on the 1st Bundesliga teams, forcing the elite teams to invest heavily in players to avoid relegation. This increased budgets 25 percent over the previous two years.

In the final Bundesliga season, 1993–94, only 11 teams wanted to play in the *2nd Bundesliga*. Furthermore, two teams folded during and after the season. Ice hockey's reputation in Germany was heavily tarnished. This made it difficult to attract serious sponsorship. In January 1994, 20 out of the remaining 21 1st and 2nd Bundesliga teams voted for creating a new entity, the DEL.

Upon founding, the "DEL Betriebsgesellschaft mbH" was the first German professional sports league managed by an organization whose members were incorporated as well. The goal behind the DEL was to create a league, based on the model of the North American [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL"), in which teams could play consistently without relegation concerns and create a stable league. Clubs in the DEL were required to conform to [rules](#League_regulations), which were designed to ensure long-term viability. Twelve clubs from the old *1st Bundesliga*, and six from the *2nd Bundesliga* came together as founding members. The new league immediately attracted corporate sponsorship with the [Krombacher Brewery](/wiki/Krombacher_Brauerei "Krombacher Brauerei"), which was prominently featured on the new league logo.

The hope of avoiding the troubles of the old *Bundesliga* by stricter financial controls did not materialize. During DEL's [initial season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga_season "1994–95 Deutsche Eishockey Liga season"), on 18 December 1994, the *Bundesliga's* final champion, the renamed *EC Hedos München*, folded. This was controversial, as DEL's president Franz Hofherr had approved their license and certified their finances. Hofherr was Mad Dogs former president and it was alleged that he must have known about their desperate financial situation.

The [Bosman ruling](/wiki/Bosman_ruling "Bosman ruling"), a 1995 decision of the [European Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice") regarding the movement of labor in soccer, had profound influence on the league. The old Bundesliga had national character with German clubs competing for the German title using mostly German players. After the ruling [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") players were excluded from the "foreign" player quota. In the [1995–96 season](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga_season "1995–96 Deutsche Eishockey Liga season") following the decision, the DEL teams employed 97 EU players. This lowered costs significantly, enabling smaller teams to compete more effectively. However, frequent player moves were not viewed positively by the fans, resulting in smaller attendance numbers.

Following an agreement with the DEB the league renamed itself as "DEL – Die 1. Bundesliga" in the [1999–2000 season](/wiki/1999%E2%80%9300_Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga_season "1999–00 Deutsche Eishockey Liga season"), while reintroducing relegation and promotion to/from the [2nd Ice Hockey Bundesliga](/wiki/2nd_Bundesliga_(ice_hockey) "2nd Bundesliga (ice hockey)").

The [2004–05 season](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga_season "2004–05 Deutsche Eishockey Liga season") was significant due to the [NHL lockout](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_lockout "2004–05 NHL lockout"). 26 NHL players came to play the season in the DEL, including [Jamie Langenbrunner](/wiki/Jamie_Langenbrunner "Jamie Langenbrunner"), [Erik Cole](/wiki/Erik_Cole "Erik Cole"), [Stéphane Robidas](/wiki/St%C3%A9phane_Robidas "Stéphane Robidas"), [Doug Weight](/wiki/Doug_Weight "Doug Weight"), [Mike York](/wiki/Mike_York "Mike York") and several German national team players – [Jochen Hecht](/wiki/Jochen_Hecht "Jochen Hecht"), [Olaf Kölzig](/wiki/Olaf_K%C3%B6lzig "Olaf Kölzig"), and [Marco Sturm](/wiki/Marco_Sturm "Marco Sturm").

## League regulations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: League regulations")]

The DEL is an independently run league, fully owned and operated by its 14 member teams. Each team must fulfill the *DEL's* basic requirements to remain in the league:

* A written application for membership;
* "On ice qualification" for new teams (championship in the 2nd Bundesliga);
* A stadium that meets DEL standards;
* Financial qualification;
* Formation of an ordinary company (the DEL consists of franchises);
* Development program for young players; and
* Purchase of a license (currently, the licensing fee is set at €800,000.00)

The DEL can only admit one 2nd Bundesliga team per season to the league, unless the league strength falls below fourteen, in which case two clubs can be admitted. Since the 2006–07 season, no DEL team can be automatically relegated, a team can only lose its league status through non-compliance with the leagues regulations (see above).

The *ESBG* guarantees to admit any DEL team wishing to step down to the lower 2nd Bundesliga or [Oberliga](/wiki/Oberliga_(ice_hockey) "Oberliga (ice hockey)"). The team, however, has to purchase a license (licensing fees for the 2nd Bundesliga are currently set at €100,000.00).

To regulate the relationship between the DEL, the *DEB* and the *ESBG* (2nd Bundesliga), a so-called *Kooperationsvertrag* exists. This *cooperation contract* was signed in December 2005, and was valid until 2011. This contract ended years of dispute between the three organizations over competencies and financial issues.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In November 2007, the DEL announced another change in policy. The league expanded to allow 16 teams beginning in the [2008–09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_DEL_season "2008–09 DEL season"), resulting in direct promotion for the [2nd Bundesliga](/wiki/2nd_Bundesliga_(ice_hockey) "2nd Bundesliga (ice hockey)") league champions, should they fulfill all requirements and be interested in joining the DEL. Should this not be the case, or a current DEL team resigns from the league, a selection process would determine the club, or clubs, who would be eligible to join in order required to achieve 16 teams. *(Note: [Füchse Duisburg](/wiki/F%C3%BCchse_Duisburg "Füchse Duisburg") resigned before the [2009–10 season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_DEL_season "2009–10 DEL season") and was not replaced until the following season.)*

For that season, it was also mandated that each DEL club would be allowed to have no more than ten non-[EC](/wiki/European_Community "European Community") players under contract.

Additionally, a new format for the game schedule will limit the number of regular-season games to 52 for each team. This is achieved by each team playing four games against eleven others and two games against the remaining four. To determine which teams play, the final standings of the previous season are used.

The DEL would also reintroduce promotion once more. The first- and second-lowest ranked teams will play a best-of-seven series to determine which team faces the [2nd Bundesliga](/wiki/2nd_Bundesliga_(ice_hockey) "2nd Bundesliga (ice hockey)") champion for a place in the league. There is, however, an ongoing dispute about those games as second division teams may only have five foreign players on contract and therefore face a handicap in comparison to the DEL teams with twelve import players each.[[4]](#cite_note-4) For now, the *ESBG* has declared that no team from the 2nd Bundesliga would take part in these matches and therefore no promotion/relegation with the DEL will take place.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-201920season_11-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-201920season_11-1) The 2019–20 season was ended prematurely due to the [coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Germany "COVID-19 pandemic in Germany"), which caused the cancellation of the play-offs. The teams are listed by their placement in the regular season.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-16poteams_12-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-16poteams_12-1) In the 1994–95 and 1995–96 seasons, 16 clubs qualified for the play-offs.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-14poteams_13-0)** In the 1997–98 season, 14 clubs qualified for the play-offs.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-202021season_14-0)** The 2020–21 playoffs were conducted under a reduced format, with no preliminary round and only eight playoff teams.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-ehcmunchen_15-0)** In 2012, *EHC München* changed their name to *EHC Red Bull München*.
6. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-iserlohn_16-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-iserlohn_16-1) In 2001, the *Starbulls Rosenheim* sold their DEL licence to the *Iserlohn Roosters*.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-dusseldorf_17-0)** In 2012, the *DEG Metro Stars* changed their name to *Düsseldorfer EG*.
8. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-hamburg_18-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-hamburg_18-1) In 2002, the *Munich Barons* relocated to become the *Hamburg Freezers*.
9. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-barons_19-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-barons_19-1) In 1999, the *Landshut Cannibals* sold their DEL licence to the *Munich Barons*.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-maddogs_20-0)** In 1995, the *Maddogs München* folded during the regular season.
